content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 11

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 11:1 -- "And the people were as those who complain of evil" vs. "Now the people became like those who complain of hardship" (va-yehi ha-am ke-mit'onenim ra be-oznei YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-was the-people as-ones-complaining-of-evil in-the-ears of YHWH; and-heard YHWH, and-burned his-anger, and-burned among-them [the]-fire of YHWH, and-consumed on-the-edge of the-camp." |
| LOGOS | "And the people were as those who complain of evil in the ears of YHWH. And YHWH heard, and his anger was kindled, and the fire of YHWH burned among them and consumed at the edge of the camp." |
| PNEUMA | "Now the people became like those who complain of hardship in YHWH's hearing, and when YHWH heard it, his anger blazed. Fire from YHWH burned among them and consumed the outskirts of the camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the people were as those who complain of evil in the ears of YHWH. And YHWH heard, and his anger was kindled, and the fire of YHWH burned among them and consumed the edge of the camp." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "Now" instead of "And" (violating binding convention for conjunctions). (b) PNEUMA renders ra as "hardship" rather than "evil"; while contextually reasonable, ra in the Hebrew is "evil" and the consensus preserves the broader term following EMET/LOGOS. (c) PNEUMA renders be-oznei YHWH as "in YHWH's hearing" rather than "in the ears of YHWH," losing the anatomical image. (d) PNEUMA says "his anger blazed" where the Hebrew va-yikhar appo uses "was kindled" (from kharah, "to burn/be hot"); "kindled" is more precise than "blazed." (e) PNEUMA says "Fire from YHWH" rather than "the fire of YHWH" -- the MT has the construct chain esh YHWH ("fire of YHWH"), not fire "from" YHWH (which follows the LXX pur para kuriou). (f) PNEUMA uses "outskirts" for bi-qetseh ("at the edge"); EMET has "on the edge," LOGOS "at the edge." The consensus uses "the edge." (g) EMET uses hyphenated gloss style throughout. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 11:2 -- "the fire sank down" vs. "the fire died down" (va-tishqa ha-esh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-cried the-people unto Moses; and-prayed Moses unto YHWH, and-sank-down the-fire." |
| LOGOS | "And the people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to YHWH, and the fire sank down." |
| PNEUMA | "The people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to YHWH, and the fire died down." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to YHWH, and the fire sank down." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders va-tishqa as "died down" rather than "sank down." The Hebrew shaqa means "to sink/subside," not "to die." "Sank down" preserves the distinctive Hebrew image (fire sinking into the ground rather than merely going out). EMET/LOGOS both have "sank down." (c) EMET uses "unto" (archaic).

---

### 11:3 -- "And he called the name" vs. "That place was named" (va-yiqra shem ha-maqom ha-hu Tav'erah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-called the-name of that place Taberah, for burned among-them [the]-fire of YHWH." |
| LOGOS | "And he called the name of that place Taberah, because the fire of YHWH burned among them." |
| PNEUMA | "That place was named Taberah, because the fire of YHWH had burned among them." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And he called the name of that place Taberah, because the fire of YHWH burned among them." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" and uses passive voice ("was named") where the Hebrew has active va-yiqra ("and he called"). (b) PNEUMA uses pluperfect "had burned" where the Hebrew has simple perfect ba'arah ("burned"). (c) EMET uses "for" for ki; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "because." Both are valid; "because" is clearer in English.

---

### 11:4 -- "the mixed multitude" vs. "the rabble" / "craved a craving" vs. "were seized by intense craving" / "Who will feed us meat?" vs. "Who will give us meat to eat?" (ve-ha-asafsuf asher be-qirbo hit'avvu ta'avah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-mixed-multitude that in-their-midst craved a-craving; and-returned and-wept also the-Sons of Israel, and-said: 'Who will-feed-us flesh?'" |
| LOGOS | "And the mixed multitude that was in their midst craved a craving, and the Sons of Israel also wept again and said, 'Who will feed us meat?'" |
| PNEUMA | "The rabble among them were seized by intense craving, and the Sons of Israel too began to weep again, saying, 'Who will give us meat to eat?'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the mixed multitude that was in their midst craved a craving, and the Sons of Israel also wept again and said, 'Who will feed us meat?'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders ha-asafsuf as "the rabble," a pejorative interpretation; "the mixed multitude" (EMET/LOGOS) is more descriptive and preserves the sense of a heterogeneous group. (c) PNEUMA renders hit'avvu ta'avah as "were seized by intense craving," replacing the cognate accusative structure with a passive phrase and adding "intense" (not in MT). The Hebrew literally says "craved a craving" and the consensus preserves this distinctive idiom. (d) PNEUMA renders mi ya'akhilenu basar as "Who will give us meat to eat?" while EMET/LOGOS have "Who will feed us meat?" The Hebrew ya'akhilenu (hiphil of akhal) means "will cause us to eat / will feed us," so "feed us" is more precise for the causative. (e) EMET uses "flesh" for basar; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "meat." The consensus uses "meat" as the contextual English equivalent.

---

### 11:5 -- "We remember" vs. "for nothing" vs. "for free" / "that we ate" vs. "we used to eat" (zakharnu et ha-dagah asher nokhal be-Mitsrayim khinnam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "We-remember the-fish that we-ate in-Egypt for-nothing; the-cucumbers and-the-melons and-the-leeks and-the-onions and-the-garlic." |
| LOGOS | "We remember the fish that we ate in Egypt for nothing, the cucumbers, and the melons, and the leeks, and the onions, and the garlic." |
| PNEUMA | "We remember the fish we used to eat in Egypt for free -- the cucumbers, the melons, the leeks, the onions, and the garlic!" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "We remember the fish that we ate in Egypt for nothing -- the cucumbers, and the melons, and the leeks, and the onions, and the garlic." -- (a) PNEUMA renders nokhal as "used to eat" (habitual aspect) where the Hebrew imperfect nokhal can be either simple past or habitual; EMET/LOGOS use "ate." The consensus uses "ate" following EMET/LOGOS. (b) PNEUMA renders khinnam as "for free" rather than "for nothing"; both are valid, but "for nothing" is more literal (EMET/LOGOS). (c) PNEUMA omits "that" before "we ate" (asher). (d) PNEUMA uses a dash before the vegetable list and an exclamation mark; EMET uses semicolon; LOGOS uses comma. The consensus uses a dash for clarity and period for punctuation. (e) PNEUMA omits the repeated "and" (ve-) before each item; the consensus preserves the polysyndeton of the Hebrew.

---

### 11:6 -- "our soul is dried up" vs. "our throats are parched" / "only toward the manna are our eyes" vs. "only this manna before our eyes" (ve-attah nafshenu yeveishah ein kol bilti el ha-man einenu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-now our-soul [is] dried-up; there-is-nothing -- except unto the-manna our-eyes [look]." |
| LOGOS | "But now our soul is dried up; there is nothing at all -- only this manna before our eyes." |
| PNEUMA | "But now our throats are parched. There is nothing at all -- nothing but this manna before our eyes." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And now our soul is dried up; there is nothing at all -- only toward the manna are our eyes." -- (a) EMET opens with "And-now" (ve-attah); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "But now." The Hebrew ve-attah begins with the conjunction ve- ("and"), so "And now" is more literal. (b) PNEUMA renders nafshenu as "our throats" rather than "our soul"; while nephesh can mean "throat/appetite," "soul" preserves the broader Hebrew term as EMET/LOGOS do. (c) PNEUMA uses "parched" for yeveishah; "dried up" (EMET/LOGOS) is closer to the Hebrew. (d) LOGOS/PNEUMA add "this manna" and "before our eyes"; the Hebrew says bilti el ha-man einenu -- "except toward the manna [are] our eyes," which EMET captures more precisely. The consensus renders the clause more literally: "only toward the manna are our eyes." (e) PNEUMA repeats "nothing" for emphasis not present in the MT.

---

### 11:7 -- "like coriander seed" vs. "like the seed of coriander" / "bdellium" vs. "bdellium resin" (ve-ha-man ke-zera gad ve-eino ke-ein ha-bedolakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-manna [was] like-the-seed of coriander, and-its-appearance like-the-appearance of bdellium." |
| LOGOS | "And the manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance like the appearance of bdellium." |
| PNEUMA | "The manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance was like the appearance of bdellium resin." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance was like the appearance of bdellium." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for ve-ha-man). (b) EMET has "the seed of coriander" (preserving the Hebrew construct ke-zera gad); LOGOS/PNEUMA have "coriander seed" (English natural order). The consensus follows the English natural order. (c) PNEUMA adds "resin" to "bdellium" -- an interpretive gloss not in the Hebrew. The identification of bedolakh as resin is probable but not certain (some propose pearl or crystal); "bdellium" alone preserves the ambiguity.

---

### 11:8 -- "ground it with millstones" vs. "ground it on the mill" vs. "grind it in hand-mills" / "cakes baked in oil" vs. "a cake of oil" vs. "rich cream" (shatu ha-am ve-laqetu... ke-ta'am leshad ha-shamen)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "They-went-about the-people and-gathered, and-ground on-the-mill or crushed in-the-mortar, and-cooked in-the-pot, and-made [et]-it [into] cakes; and-was its-taste like-the-taste of a-cake of oil." |
| LOGOS | "The people went about and gathered it and ground it with millstones or crushed it in the mortar and boiled it in the pot and made cakes of it; and its taste was like the taste of cakes baked in oil." |
| PNEUMA | "The people would go about and gather it, then grind it in hand-mills or crush it in mortars. They would cook it in pots or make it into flat cakes. Its taste was like the taste of rich cream." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The people went about and gathered it and ground it with millstones or crushed it in the mortar, and cooked it in the pot and made cakes of it; and its taste was like the taste of a cake of oil." -- (a) PNEUMA uses habitual "would go about and gather" where the Hebrew shatu ... ve-laqetu uses simple perfects; EMET/LOGOS use simple past. (b) EMET has "on the mill" (ba-rekhayim); LOGOS has "with millstones"; PNEUMA has "in hand-mills." The Hebrew rekhayim is a dual form meaning "a pair of millstones" or "hand-mill"; "with millstones" captures this. (c) LOGOS renders u-visshelu as "boiled" where EMET/PNEUMA have "cooked"; the Hebrew bishshel means "to cook/boil," and "cooked" is the broader term. The consensus uses "cooked." (d) PNEUMA adds "flat" to "cakes" and "into" -- interpretive expansions. (e) The critical divergence is leshad ha-shamen: EMET has "a cake of oil," LOGOS has "cakes baked in oil," and PNEUMA has "rich cream." The Hebrew leshad is debated (possibly "moisture/richness" or a type of pastry), and ha-shamen means "the oil." PNEUMA's "rich cream" is a dynamic interpretation that departs significantly from the Hebrew. LOGOS's "cakes baked in oil" adds "baked" (not in MT). The consensus follows EMET's more literal "a cake of oil."

---

### 11:9 -- "when the dew fell" vs. "when the dew descended" / "the manna fell upon it" vs. "the manna would fall with it" (u-ve-redeth ha-tal al ha-makhaneh lailah yered ha-man alav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-when-descended the-dew upon the-camp at-night, descended the-manna upon-it." |
| LOGOS | "And when the dew fell upon the camp at night, the manna fell upon it." |
| PNEUMA | "When the dew fell on the camp at night, the manna would fall with it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when the dew fell upon the camp at night, the manna fell upon it." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention for u-ve-redeth). (b) EMET uses "descended" for yered (more literal); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "fell." "Fell" is standard English for dew. (c) PNEUMA has "on" rather than "upon" for al; minor variation. (d) PNEUMA renders yered ha-man alav as "the manna would fall with it" -- using habitual "would fall" (not in Hebrew) and "with it" rather than "upon it" (alav = "upon it," not "with it"). The Hebrew says the manna descended upon the camp, not that it fell together with the dew.

---

### 11:10 -- "weeping by their families" vs. "weeping according to their families" vs. "weeping, family after family" / "it was evil" vs. "it was a terrible thing" (bokheh le-mishpekhotav ish le-petakh oholo)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-heard Moses the-people weeping by-their-families, each [at]-the-entrance of his-tent; and-burned the-anger of YHWH exceedingly; and-in-the-eyes of Moses [it was] evil." |
| LOGOS | "And Moses heard the people weeping according to their families, each man at the entrance of his tent. And the anger of YHWH was kindled greatly, and in the eyes of Moses it was evil." |
| PNEUMA | "Moses heard the people weeping, family after family, each one at the entrance of his tent. YHWH's anger blazed fiercely, and it was a terrible thing in Moses' eyes." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses heard the people weeping by their families, each man at the entrance of his tent. And the anger of YHWH was kindled greatly, and in the eyes of Moses it was evil." -- (a) PNEUMA omits both opening "And" conjunctions (binding convention). (b) EMET has "by their families"; LOGOS has "according to their families"; PNEUMA has "family after family." The Hebrew le-mishpekhotav = "by/according to their families"; "by their families" is concise and accurate. (c) PNEUMA has "each one" where Hebrew has ish ("each man"). (d) PNEUMA renders va-yikhar af YHWH me'od as "YHWH's anger blazed fiercely" where the Hebrew uses "the anger of YHWH was kindled greatly" (LOGOS). "Kindled" is more precise for kharah than "blazed." (e) PNEUMA renders u-ve-einei Mosheh ra as "it was a terrible thing in Moses' eyes" -- paraphrasing ra ("evil") as "a terrible thing" and restructuring. The consensus preserves the Hebrew word order and the term "evil."

---

### 11:11 -- "Why have you done evil to your servant?" vs. "Why have you brought this misery on your servant?" / "that you lay the burden" vs. "to put the burden" (lamah hare'ota le-avdekha ve-lammah lo matsati khen be-einekha lasum et massa kol ha-am ha-zeh alai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Why have-you-done-evil to-your-servant? And-why have-I-not found favor in-your-eyes, to-put the-burden of all this people upon-me?" |
| LOGOS | "Why have you done evil to your servant? And why have I not found favor in your eyes, that you lay the burden of all this people upon me?" |
| PNEUMA | "Why have you brought this misery on your servant? What have I done to displease you, that you have laid the burden of this entire people on me?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Why have you done evil to your servant? And why have I not found favor in your eyes, that you lay the burden of all this people upon me?" -- (a) PNEUMA renders hare'ota as "brought this misery on" rather than "done evil to"; the Hebrew hiphil of ra'a literally means "to do evil to/cause evil to." "Done evil to" preserves the bold directness of Moses' accusation against YHWH. (b) PNEUMA renders the second question as "What have I done to displease you?" -- a significant restructuring of ve-lammah lo matsati khen be-einekha ("and why have I not found favor in your eyes?"). (c) PNEUMA says "this entire people" where the Hebrew says kol ha-am ha-zeh ("all this people"). (d) EMET has "to put" for lasum; LOGOS has "that you lay." "That you lay" better conveys the purpose clause in English. (e) PNEUMA uses "on me" rather than "upon me" (alai); minor.

---

### 11:12 -- "Carry them in your bosom" vs. "Carry them in your arms" / "the nursing father" vs. "a nursing father" vs. "a nursing father" / "the suckling child" vs. "the suckling" vs. "an infant" / "to the land" vs. "to the ground" (sa'ehu be-kheiqekha ka-asher yissa ha-omen et ha-yoneq al ha-adamah asher nishba'ta la-avotav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Did-I conceive all this people? Or-did-I give-birth-to-him, that you-say unto-me: 'Carry-him in-your-bosom, as carries the-nursing-one the-suckling, unto the-ground that you-swore to-his-fathers'?" |
| LOGOS | "Did I conceive all this people? Did I give them birth, that you should say to me, 'Carry them in your bosom, as the nursing father carries the suckling child,' to the land that you swore to their fathers?" |
| PNEUMA | "Did I conceive this entire people? Did I give birth to them, that you should say to me, 'Carry them in your arms, the way a nursing father carries an infant' -- all the way to the land you swore to give their ancestors?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Did I conceive all this people? Did I give birth to them, that you should say to me, 'Carry them in your bosom, as the nursing father carries the suckling child, to the land that you swore to their fathers'?" -- (a) EMET has "Or did I" for im-anokhi; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "Did I." Both are possible; the consensus uses "Did I." (b) PNEUMA has "in your arms" for be-kheiqekha; the Hebrew kheiq means "bosom/lap," not "arms." EMET/LOGOS have "bosom." (c) PNEUMA has "the way" for ka-asher; "as" is more literal. (d) EMET has "the suckling" for ha-yoneq; LOGOS has "the suckling child"; PNEUMA has "an infant." The Hebrew yoneq means "suckling/nursing one" -- "suckling child" captures both the nursing aspect and the identity. (e) PNEUMA adds "all the way" (not in Hebrew). (f) PNEUMA renders ha-adamah as "the land" and adds "to give" (not in Hebrew nishba'ta, "you swore"); EMET has "the ground" (more literal for adamah). LOGOS has "the land." The consensus uses "the land" since the context is the promised land, but notes adamah literally means "ground/soil." (g) PNEUMA has "their ancestors" for la-avotav; "their fathers" (EMET/LOGOS) is more literal for avot. (h) EMET uses "unto me" and "unto the ground" (archaic).

---

### 11:13 -- "From where shall I get meat" vs. "Where am I supposed to get meat" / "they weep upon me" vs. "they weep before me" vs. "they keep weeping at me" (me-ayin li basar latet le-khol ha-am ha-zeh ki yivku alai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "From-where to-me flesh to-give to-all this people? For they-weep upon-me, saying: 'Give to-us flesh, and-we-will-eat.'" |
| LOGOS | "From where shall I get meat to give to all this people? For they weep before me, saying, 'Give us meat that we may eat.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Where am I supposed to get meat for all these people? They keep weeping at me, saying, 'Give us meat to eat!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "From where shall I get meat to give to all this people? For they weep upon me, saying, 'Give us meat that we may eat.'" -- (a) PNEUMA restructures me-ayin li as "Where am I supposed to get" -- adding "supposed" (not in Hebrew). EMET/LOGOS preserve "From where." (b) PNEUMA omits "For" (ki) before "they weep." (c) EMET has "upon me" for alai; LOGOS has "before me"; PNEUMA has "at me." The Hebrew alai = "upon me"; the consensus follows EMET's more literal reading. (d) PNEUMA has "keep weeping" (continuous aspect not explicit in Hebrew). (e) LOGOS has "that we may eat" (ve-nokhelah, cohortative); PNEUMA has "to eat"; EMET has "and we will eat." "That we may eat" captures the cohortative best. (f) EMET uses "flesh" for basar; the consensus uses "meat."

---

### 11:14 -- "I am not able to carry" vs. "I cannot carry" / "for it is too heavy for me" vs. "it is too heavy for me" (lo ukhal anokhi levaddi laset et kol ha-am ha-zeh ki kaved mimmeni)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Not I-am-able I alone to-carry all this people, for heavy [it is] from-me." |
| LOGOS | "I am not able to bear all this people alone, for it is too heavy for me." |
| PNEUMA | "I cannot carry this entire people by myself -- it is too heavy for me!" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I am not able to carry all this people alone, for it is too heavy for me." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "cannot" for lo ukhal, a contraction; EMET/LOGOS preserve the formal "I am not able." (b) LOGOS uses "bear" for laset; EMET/PNEUMA use "carry." Both are valid for nasa; the consensus uses "carry" (majority). (c) PNEUMA uses "this entire people" for kol ha-am ha-zeh; "all this people" is more literal. (d) PNEUMA uses "by myself" for levaddi; "alone" (EMET/LOGOS) is more literal. (e) PNEUMA uses a dash and exclamation mark; the Hebrew has no such emphatic marker. (f) PNEUMA omits "for" (ki).

---

### 11:15 -- "kill me, I pray -- utterly" vs. "please kill me altogether" vs. "kill me right now" / "my wretchedness" vs. "my evil" vs. "my own ruin" (horgeni na harog im matsati khen be-einekha ve-al er'eh be-ra'ati)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-if thus you are-doing to-me, kill-me now -- killing -- if I-have-found favor in-your-eyes, and-let-me-not see in-my-evil." |
| LOGOS | "And if thus you are doing to me, please kill me altogether, if I have found favor in your eyes, and let me not see my evil." |
| PNEUMA | "If this is how you are going to treat me, then kill me right now -- if I have found any favor in your sight -- and do not let me see my own ruin." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if thus you are doing to me, kill me, I pray -- utterly -- if I have found favor in your eyes, and let me not see my wretchedness." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention) and paraphrases kakhah at oseh li as "this is how you are going to treat me" (future tense and informal register not in Hebrew). (b) The phrase horgeni na harog is difficult: na = "please/I pray," harog is the infinitive absolute intensifying the imperative. EMET renders it "kill me now -- killing"; LOGOS has "please kill me altogether"; PNEUMA has "kill me right now." The consensus renders na as "I pray" and the infinitive absolute as "utterly" to capture the intensification. (c) PNEUMA adds "any" before "favor" and uses "in your sight" rather than "in your eyes" (be-einekha). (d) PNEUMA renders be-ra'ati as "my own ruin"; EMET has "my evil"; LOGOS has "my evil." The Hebrew ra'ah here means "wretchedness/misery" rather than moral evil. The consensus uses "wretchedness" to distinguish from moral evil while remaining close to the Hebrew.

---

### 11:16 -- "Gather for me" / "whom you know to be" vs. "whom you know that they are" / "bring them" vs. "you shall take them" / "let them station themselves" vs. "have them take their stand" (esfah li shiv'im ish mi-ziqnei Yisra'el asher yadata ki hem ziqnei ha-am ve-shoteriv ve-laqakhta otam el Ohel Mo'ed ve-hityatsvu sham immakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Gather to-me seventy men from-the-elders of Israel, whom you-know that they [are] elders of the-people and-its-officers; and-you-shall-take them unto the-Tent of Meeting, and-they-shall-station-themselves there with-you." |
| LOGOS | "Gather for me seventy men from the elders of Israel, whom you know to be the elders of the people and their officers, and bring them to the tent of meeting, and let them station themselves there with you." |
| PNEUMA | "Gather for me seventy men from the elders of Israel -- men you know to be elders and officers of the people. Bring them to the Tent of Meeting and have them take their stand there with you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Gather for me seventy men from the elders of Israel, whom you know to be the elders of the people and their officers; and bring them to the Tent of Meeting, and let them station themselves there with you." -- (a) EMET has "to me" for li; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "for me." Both are valid; "for me" is more natural English. (b) PNEUMA restructures the relative clause with dashes and drops "whom." (c) EMET has "you shall take" for ve-laqakhta; LOGOS has "bring"; PNEUMA has "Bring." The Hebrew laqakh means "to take," but "bring" is the natural English rendering of taking someone to a destination. (d) PNEUMA has "have them take their stand" for ve-hityatsvu; EMET/LOGOS have "let them station themselves." The Hebrew hitpael of yatsav means "to station oneself/take one's stand"; "let them station themselves" preserves the reflexive. (e) LOGOS uses lowercase "tent of meeting"; the consensus capitalizes per convention.

---

### 11:17 -- "I will take of the spirit" vs. "I will take some of the spirit" / "they shall bear the burden" vs. "they can share the burden" (ve-atsalti min ha-ruakh asher alekha ve-samti aleihem ve-nas'u ittekha be-massa ha-am)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-I-will-come-down and-I-will-speak with-you there, and-I-will-take from the-spirit that [is] upon-you, and-I-will-put upon-them; and-they-shall-carry with-you in-the-burden of the-people, and-not you-shall-carry you alone." |
| LOGOS | "And I will come down and speak with you there, and I will take of the Spirit that is upon you and put it upon them, and they shall bear the burden of the people with you, and you shall not bear it alone." |
| PNEUMA | "I will come down and speak with you there. I will take some of the spirit that is on you and place it on them, so that they can share the burden of this people with you, and you will not have to carry it alone." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And I will come down and speak with you there, and I will take of the spirit that is upon you and put it upon them; and they shall bear the burden of the people with you, and you shall not bear it alone." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA adds "some of" to render the partitive min; EMET has "from"; LOGOS has "of." The English "of" (as in "I will take of the spirit") preserves the partitive sense elegantly. (c) LOGOS capitalizes "Spirit"; EMET/PNEUMA use lowercase. The Hebrew ruakh has no capitalization; the consensus uses lowercase "spirit" since this is not a distinct theological entity but the spirit already on Moses. (d) PNEUMA has "place it on them" for ve-samti aleihem; EMET/LOGOS have "put upon them." "Put" is more literal for sim. (e) PNEUMA restructures ve-nas'u ittekha be-massa ha-am as "so that they can share the burden of this people with you" -- adding "so that" (purpose clause not explicit in Hebrew) and "can share" (the Hebrew says "they shall carry/bear"). (f) PNEUMA has "you will not have to carry" for ve-lo tissa; "you shall not bear" preserves the jussive force. (g) EMET has "they shall carry" (nasa); LOGOS has "they shall bear." "Bear" is used in the consensus to match "bear" in the second clause.

---

### 11:18 -- "Consecrate yourselves" vs. "Sanctify yourselves" / "Who will feed us meat?" vs. "Who will give us meat to eat?" (hitqaddeshu le-makhar va-akhaltem basar)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-unto the-people you-shall-say: 'Sanctify-yourselves for-tomorrow, and-you-shall-eat flesh...'" |
| LOGOS | "And to the people you shall say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, and you shall eat meat...'" |
| PNEUMA | "And to the people you are to say: 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, because you will eat meat...'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And to the people you shall say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, and you shall eat meat...'" -- (a) EMET uses "Sanctify" for hitqaddeshu; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "Consecrate." Both translate the hitpael of qadash; "Consecrate" is the majority rendering. (b) PNEUMA uses "you are to say" rather than "you shall say" for tomar; "shall" preserves the imperative force. (c) PNEUMA uses "because" and "you will eat" where the Hebrew has "and you shall eat" (va-akhaltem with consecutive vav). (d) EMET uses "flesh" for basar.

---

### 11:19 -- "Not one day shall you eat" vs. "Not for one day will you eat it" (lo yom ekhad tokhelun ve-lo yomayim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Not a-day one you-shall-eat, and-not two days, and-not five days, and-not ten days, and-not twenty days;" |
| LOGOS | "You shall not eat one day, nor two days, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days," |
| PNEUMA | "Not for one day will you eat it, not for two days, not for five days, not for ten days, not for twenty days --" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Not one day shall you eat, nor two days, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days," -- (a) The Hebrew word order is lo yom ekhad tokhelun -- "not a day one shall you eat." EMET preserves this inverted order. LOGOS restructures to "You shall not eat one day." PNEUMA adds "for" and "it" (not in Hebrew). The consensus follows the Hebrew emphasis with "not" fronted. (b) EMET uses "and not" for each ve-lo; LOGOS uses "nor"; PNEUMA uses "not for." "Nor" is the standard English correlative after an initial negation. (c) PNEUMA adds "for" before each time period (not in Hebrew).

---

### 11:20 -- "a whole month of days" vs. "for a whole month" / "becomes loathsome" vs. "becomes a loathsome thing" / "Why is it that we came out" vs. "Why did we ever leave" (ad khodesh yamim ad asher yetse me-appekhem ve-hayah lakhem le-zara)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Until a-month of days, until it-comes-out from-your-nostrils, and-it-becomes to-you as-a-loathsome-thing; because you-have-rejected YHWH who [is] in-your-midst, and-you-wept before-him, saying: 'Why this did-we-come-out from-Egypt?'" |
| LOGOS | "but a whole month of days, until it comes out of your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you, because you have rejected YHWH who is in your midst and have wept before him, saying, 'Why is it that we came out of Egypt?'" |
| PNEUMA | "but for a whole month, until it comes out of your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you -- because you have rejected YHWH who is in your midst, and you have wept before him, saying, 'Why did we ever leave Egypt?'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "but a whole month of days, until it comes out of your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you, because you have rejected YHWH who is in your midst, and you have wept before him, saying, 'Why is it that we came out of Egypt?'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits "of days" (yamim) from "month of days" (khodesh yamim). (b) EMET has "as a loathsome thing" for le-zara; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "loathsome to you." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's more natural rendering. (c) PNEUMA renders lammah zeh yatsanu mi-Mitsrayim as "Why did we ever leave Egypt?" -- adding "ever" and using "leave" rather than "came out" (yatsanu from yatsa, "to go out/come out"). "Came out of Egypt" preserves the exodus-specific verb. (d) EMET uses "from Egypt" for mi-Mitsrayim; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "of Egypt." "Of Egypt" is standard.

---

### 11:21 -- "Six hundred thousand footmen" vs. "six hundred thousand on foot" / "in whose midst I am" vs. "I am among" (shesh me'ot elef ragli ha-am asher anokhi be-qirbo)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Six hundred thousand on-foot the-people that I [am] in-their-midst; and-you have-said: 'Flesh I-will-give to-them, and-they-shall-eat a-month of days.'" |
| LOGOS | "Six hundred thousand footmen are the people in whose midst I am, and you have said, 'I will give them meat, and they shall eat a whole month of days.'" |
| PNEUMA | "The people I am among number six hundred thousand on foot, and you say, 'I will give them meat and they will eat for a whole month'?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Six hundred thousand footmen are the people in whose midst I am, and you have said, 'I will give them meat, and they shall eat a whole month of days.'" -- (a) PNEUMA restructures the entire clause, fronting "The people I am among" and using "number" (not in Hebrew). (b) EMET has "on foot" for ragli; LOGOS has "footmen"; PNEUMA has "on foot." The Hebrew ragli is an adjective meaning "on foot/foot-soldier"; "footmen" captures the substantive use. (c) PNEUMA omits "of days" (yamim) from khodesh yamim. (d) PNEUMA uses "you say" (present) for amarta (perfect "you have said"). (e) EMET uses "flesh."

---

### 11:22 -- "Shall flocks and herds be slaughtered" vs. "If flocks and herds were slaughtered" / "that it should suffice" vs. "would that be enough" (ha-tson u-vaqar yishshakhet lahem u-matsa lahem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Will-flocks and-herds be-slaughtered for-them, and-will-it-suffice for-them? Or all the-fish of the-sea will-be-gathered for-them, and-will-it-suffice for-them?" |
| LOGOS | "Shall flocks and herds be slaughtered for them, that it should suffice for them? Or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered for them, that it should suffice for them?" |
| PNEUMA | "If flocks and herds were slaughtered for them, would that be enough? If all the fish in the sea were gathered for them, would that be enough?" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Shall flocks and herds be slaughtered for them, that it should suffice for them? Or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered for them, that it should suffice for them?" -- (a) PNEUMA restructures both questions as conditional ("If... were... would...") rather than interrogative ("Shall... be..."). The Hebrew uses interrogative particles (ha-) and imperfect verbs, which English interrogative "Shall" captures. (b) PNEUMA renders u-matsa lahem as "would that be enough?" rather than "that it should suffice for them." The Hebrew matsa ("to find/suffice") is rendered more precisely by "suffice." (c) PNEUMA has "fish in the sea" rather than "fish of the sea" (degei ha-yam, a construct chain: "fish of the sea").

---

### 11:23 -- "Is the hand of YHWH too short?" vs. "Is YHWH's arm too short?" / "whether my word will come to pass for you" vs. "whether my word comes true for you" (ha-yad YHWH tiqtsar attah tir'eh ha-yiqrekha devari im lo)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Is-the-hand of YHWH too-short? Now you-shall-see whether-will-happen my-word to-you or not." |
| LOGOS | "Is the hand of YHWH too short? Now you shall see whether my word will come to pass for you or not." |
| PNEUMA | "Is YHWH's arm too short? Now you will see whether my word comes true for you or not." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Is the hand of YHWH too short? Now you shall see whether my word will come to pass for you or not." -- (a) PNEUMA renders yad as "arm" rather than "hand"; the Hebrew yad specifically means "hand," not zero'a ("arm"). (b) PNEUMA uses possessive "YHWH's arm" rather than the Hebrew construct "the hand of YHWH" (yad YHWH). (c) PNEUMA has "you will see" for tir'eh; EMET/LOGOS have "you shall see." "Shall" preserves the force of the divine declaration. (d) PNEUMA renders ha-yiqrekha devari as "my word comes true" -- a dynamic rendering of qarah ("to encounter/happen/befall"). LOGOS has "come to pass" and EMET has "will happen." "Come to pass" is the established English biblical idiom.

---

### 11:24 -- "spoke to the people the words of YHWH" vs. "told the people what YHWH had said" (va-yedabber el ha-am et divrei YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-went-out Moses and-spoke unto the-people the-words of YHWH; and-gathered seventy men from-the-elders of the-people, and-stationed them around the-Tent." |
| LOGOS | "And Moses went out and spoke to the people the words of YHWH. And he gathered seventy men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the tent." |
| PNEUMA | "Moses went out and told the people what YHWH had said. He gathered seventy men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the Tent." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses went out and spoke to the people the words of YHWH. And he gathered seventy men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the Tent." -- (a) PNEUMA omits both "And" conjunctions (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders va-yedabber et divrei YHWH as "told the people what YHWH had said" -- a paraphrase replacing "spoke...the words of YHWH" with indirect speech. (c) LOGOS uses lowercase "tent"; the consensus capitalizes per convention.

---

### 11:25 -- "he took of the spirit" vs. "He took some of the spirit" / "they prophesied, but they did not continue" vs. "they prophesied -- but they did not do so again" (va-yatsel min ha-ruakh asher alav va-yitten al shiv'im ish ha-zeqenim va-yitnabe'u ve-lo yasafu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-came-down YHWH in-the-cloud, and-spoke unto-him; and-he-took from the-spirit that [was] upon-him, and-gave upon the-seventy men the-elders; and-it-was when-rested upon-them the-spirit, and-they-prophesied, and-not they-continued." |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH came down in the cloud and spoke to him, and he took of the Spirit that was upon him and put it upon the seventy men, the elders. And it came to pass, when the Spirit rested upon them, that they prophesied, but they did not continue." |
| PNEUMA | "Then YHWH came down in the cloud and spoke with him. He took some of the spirit that was on Moses and placed it on the seventy elders. When the spirit rested on them, they prophesied -- but they did not do so again." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH came down in the cloud and spoke to him, and he took of the spirit that was upon him and put it upon the seventy men, the elders. And it came to pass, when the spirit rested upon them, that they prophesied, but they did not continue." -- (a) PNEUMA opens with "Then" instead of "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA adds "some of" for the partitive min; "of" suffices in English. (c) PNEUMA specifies "on Moses" where the Hebrew has "upon him" (alav); the consensus preserves the pronoun. (d) PNEUMA has "placed it on" for va-yitten al; "put upon" is closer to natan ("to give/put"). (e) PNEUMA omits the va-yehi construction ("And it came to pass"), a characteristic Hebrew narrative marker. (f) PNEUMA has "they did not do so again" for ve-lo yasafu; EMET has "they did not continue"; LOGOS has "they did not continue." "Did not continue" is the straightforward rendering of yasaf. (g) LOGOS capitalizes "Spirit"; the consensus uses lowercase.

---

### 11:26 -- "And two men had remained" / "among those written down" vs. "among those registered" / "they had not gone out to the Tent" vs. "they had not gone out to the tent" (va-yishsha'aru shnei anashim ba-makhaneh... ba-ketuvim... ve-lo yats'u el ha-Ohelah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-remained two men in-the-camp; the-name of the-one Eldad, and-the-name of the-second Medad; and-rested upon-them the-spirit -- and-they [were] among-the-ones-written, and-not they-went-out unto the-Tent; and-they-prophesied in-the-camp." |
| LOGOS | "And two men had remained in the camp; the name of the one was Eldad, and the name of the second was Medad. And the Spirit rested upon them -- and they were among those written down, but they had not gone out to the tent -- and they prophesied in the camp." |
| PNEUMA | "Two men had remained in the camp; the name of one was Eldad and the name of the other was Medad. The spirit rested on them as well -- they were among those registered, but they had not gone out to the Tent -- and they prophesied right there in the camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And two men had remained in the camp; the name of the one was Eldad, and the name of the second was Medad. And the spirit rested upon them -- and they were among those written down, but they had not gone out to the Tent -- and they prophesied in the camp." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA has "the other" for ha-sheni; the Hebrew means "the second," as EMET/LOGOS render it. (c) PNEUMA adds "as well" after "rested on them" (not in Hebrew). (d) PNEUMA renders ba-ketuvim as "among those registered" rather than "among those written down" (LOGOS) or "among the ones written" (EMET). The Hebrew katav means "to write"; "written down" is more literal. (e) PNEUMA adds "right there" before "in the camp" (not in Hebrew). (f) LOGOS capitalizes "Spirit"; consensus uses lowercase.

---

### 11:27 -- "And a young man ran and told Moses" vs. "A young man ran and reported to Moses" (va-yarats ha-na'ar va-yagged le-Mosheh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-ran the-young-man and-told to-Moses, and-said: 'Eldad and-Medad are-prophesying in-the-camp.'" |
| LOGOS | "And a young man ran and told Moses, and said, 'Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp.'" |
| PNEUMA | "A young man ran and reported to Moses, 'Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And a young man ran and told Moses, and said, 'Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp.'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA uses "reported" for va-yagged; "told" (EMET/LOGOS) is simpler and sufficient for nagad. (c) PNEUMA omits "and said" (va-yomer), collapsing the two verbs into one. The Hebrew distinctly has both va-yagged ("and he told") and va-yomer ("and he said"). (d) PNEUMA adds an exclamation mark.

---

### 11:28 -- "the attendant of Moses from his chosen ones" vs. "who had served Moses since his youth" / "restrain them" vs. "stop them" (mesharet Mosheh mi-bekhurav... kela'em)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-answered Joshua son of Nun, the-attendant of Moses, from-his-chosen-ones, and-said: 'My-lord Moses, restrain-them!'" |
| LOGOS | "And Joshua the son of Nun, the attendant of Moses from his chosen ones, answered and said, 'My lord Moses, restrain them!'" |
| PNEUMA | "Joshua son of Nun, who had served Moses since his youth, spoke up and said, 'My lord Moses, stop them!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Joshua the son of Nun, the attendant of Moses from his chosen ones, answered and said, 'My lord Moses, restrain them!'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders mesharet Mosheh mi-bekhurav as "who had served Moses since his youth" -- reading mi-bekhurav as "from his youth" rather than "from his chosen ones." This is one legitimate interpretation, but the Masoretic vocalization supports "from his chosen ones" (bakhur = "chosen/select young man"), which EMET/LOGOS follow. (c) PNEUMA uses "spoke up" for va-ya'an ("answered"); "answered" is the standard rendering. (d) PNEUMA uses "stop them" for kela'em; EMET/LOGOS use "restrain them." The Hebrew kala means "to restrain/imprison/withhold" -- a stronger word than "stop." "Restrain" captures this force.

---

### 11:29 -- "Are you jealous for my sake?" vs. "Are you jealous for me?" / "Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets" vs. "If only all of YHWH's people were prophets" (ha-meqanne attah li u-mi yitten kol am YHWH nevi'im ki yitten YHWH et rukho aleihem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Are-you-jealous you for-me? And-who-would-give [that] all the-people of YHWH [were] prophets, that would-give YHWH his-spirit upon-them!" |
| LOGOS | "Are you jealous for my sake? Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would put his Spirit upon them!" |
| PNEUMA | "Are you jealous on my behalf? If only all of YHWH's people were prophets -- if only YHWH would put his spirit on every one of them!" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Are you jealous for my sake? Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would put his spirit upon them!" -- (a) EMET has "for me" for li; LOGOS has "for my sake"; PNEUMA has "on my behalf." The Hebrew li = "for me/on my account." "For my sake" captures the sense of jealousy exercised on behalf of another. (b) PNEUMA renders u-mi yitten as "If only" rather than the more literal "Would that" (= "who would give"). "Would that" preserves the Hebrew optative formula mi yitten. (c) PNEUMA restructures "all the people of YHWH" as "all of YHWH's people" (possessive shift). (d) PNEUMA adds "every one of" before "them" (not in Hebrew aleihem, "upon them"). (e) PNEUMA uses "if only" twice, splitting the Hebrew into two parallel wishes; the Hebrew has a single mi yitten governing both clauses. (f) LOGOS capitalizes "Spirit"; the consensus uses lowercase.

---

### 11:30 -- "And Moses gathered himself into the camp" vs. "Then Moses withdrew to the camp" (va-ye'asef Mosheh el ha-makhaneh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-gathered Moses unto the-camp, he and-the-elders of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And Moses gathered himself into the camp, he and the elders of Israel." |
| PNEUMA | "Then Moses withdrew to the camp, he and the elders of Israel." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses gathered himself into the camp, he and the elders of Israel." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "Then" instead of "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders va-ye'asef as "withdrew"; the Hebrew niphal of asaf means "to gather oneself/withdraw." LOGOS's "gathered himself" is more literal, preserving the reflexive niphal. (c) EMET omits the reflexive element ("gathered Moses"). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 11:31 -- "and it drove quail from the sea and cast them upon the camp" vs. "and it drove quail from the sea and let them fall beside the camp" vs. "and drove quail in from the sea. It brought them down near the camp" (va-yagaz salvav min ha-yam va-yittosh al ha-makhaneh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-a-wind journeyed from-with YHWH, and-cut-off quail from the-sea; and-cast upon the-camp as-a-journey of a-day thus, and-as-a-journey of a-day thus, all-around the-camp; and-about-two-cubits upon the-face of the-ground." |
| LOGOS | "And a wind went out from YHWH, and it drove quail from the sea and let them fall beside the camp, about a day's journey on this side and about a day's journey on the other side, around the camp, and about two cubits above the face of the ground." |
| PNEUMA | "A wind went out from YHWH and drove quail in from the sea. It brought them down near the camp, about a day's journey on one side and a day's journey on the other, all around the camp, and about two cubits deep on the surface of the ground." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And a wind went out from YHWH, and it drove quail from the sea and cast them upon the camp, about a day's journey on this side and about a day's journey on that side, all around the camp, and about two cubits above the face of the ground." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) EMET has "journeyed" for nasa; "went out" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is clearer for the wind's movement. (c) EMET has "cut off" for va-yagaz (most literal); LOGOS/PNEUMA have "drove." The consensus uses "drove" for clarity with quail. (d) The critical verb va-yittosh (from natash, "to leave/abandon/cast"): EMET has "cast"; LOGOS has "let them fall"; PNEUMA has "brought them down." "Cast" is closest to the Hebrew natash in the sense of "to fling/hurl down." (e) LOGOS has "beside the camp"; PNEUMA has "near the camp"; the Hebrew says al ha-makhaneh ("upon the camp"). The consensus uses "upon." (f) EMET has "thus...thus" for koh...koh; LOGOS has "this side...other side"; PNEUMA has "one side...the other." The Hebrew koh = "thus/this way"; the consensus uses "this side...that side." (g) The crucial issue: "about two cubits above the face of the ground" vs. "about two cubits deep on the surface of the ground." PNEUMA interprets ke-ammatayim al penei ha-arets as describing depth of accumulation, while EMET/LOGOS take it as flight altitude. The Hebrew al penei ("upon the face of") supports height above the surface. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 11:32 -- "He who gathered least gathered ten homers" vs. "The one who gathered least collected ten homers" (ha-mam'it asaf asarah khomarim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-rose the-people all that day and-all the-night and-all the-day of the-morrow, and-gathered the-quail; the-one-who-did-least gathered ten homers; and-they-spread for-themselves spreading around the-camp." |
| LOGOS | "And the people rose up all that day and all the night and all the next day, and gathered the quail. He who gathered least gathered ten homers. And they spread them out for themselves all around the camp." |
| PNEUMA | "The people were up all that day and all that night and all the next day gathering the quail. The one who gathered least collected ten homers. They spread them out for themselves all around the camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the people rose up all that day and all the night and all the next day, and gathered the quail. He who gathered least gathered ten homers. And they spread them out for themselves all around the camp." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA has "were up" for va-yaqam; "rose up" (EMET/LOGOS) is more literal for qum. (c) PNEUMA has "all that night" where EMET/LOGOS have "all the night" (kol ha-lailah); the Hebrew has the definite article, not the demonstrative. (d) PNEUMA restructures "and gathered" into a participle "gathering" -- changing the Hebrew's coordinate clause into a subordinate phrase. (e) PNEUMA has "collected" for asaf; EMET/LOGOS have "gathered." "Gathered" maintains the verbal echo with the chapter's gathering theme. (f) PNEUMA omits "And" before "They spread."

---

### 11:33 -- "before it was cut off" vs. "before it was chewed" vs. "they had not yet chewed it" (terem yikkaret)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "The-flesh was-still between their-teeth, before it-was-cut-off; and-the-anger of YHWH burned against-the-people, and-struck YHWH among-the-people a-plague exceedingly great." |
| LOGOS | "The meat was still between their teeth, before it was chewed, when the anger of YHWH was kindled against the people, and YHWH struck the people with a very great plague." |
| PNEUMA | "The meat was still between their teeth -- they had not yet chewed it -- when YHWH's anger blazed against the people, and YHWH struck the people with a devastating plague." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The meat was still between their teeth, before it was cut off, when the anger of YHWH was kindled against the people, and YHWH struck the people with an exceedingly great plague." -- (a) The phrase terem yikkaret: EMET has "before it was cut off" (literal for karat, "to cut"); LOGOS has "before it was chewed" (interpretive); PNEUMA restructures as "they had not yet chewed it." The Hebrew yikkaret (niphal of karat, "to cut off") literally means "before it was cut off," which may mean before the supply was exhausted or before the meat was bitten through. The consensus preserves the literal "cut off" and lets the reader assess the image. (b) PNEUMA uses "YHWH's anger blazed" for va-af YHWH kharah; "the anger of YHWH was kindled" preserves both the construct chain and the verb kharah. (c) PNEUMA has "devastating plague" for makkah rabbah me'od; EMET has "plague exceedingly great"; LOGOS has "very great plague." The Hebrew me'od rabbah = "exceedingly great." "Devastating" is a dynamic interpretation. The consensus uses "exceedingly great" to capture both modifiers. (d) EMET uses "flesh" for basar.

---

### 11:34 -- "Kibroth-hattaavah" vs. "Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah" / "the people who had the craving" vs. "the people who had been seized by craving" (ki sham qaveru et ha-am ha-mit'avvim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-called the-name of that place Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah, for there they-buried the-people the-ones-craving." |
| LOGOS | "And he called the name of that place Kibroth-hattaavah, because there they buried the people who had the craving." |
| PNEUMA | "That place was named Kibroth-hattaavah, because there they buried the people who had been seized by craving." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And he called the name of that place Kibroth-hattaavah, because there they buried the people who had the craving." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" and uses passive "was named" where the Hebrew has active va-yiqra ("and he called"), same issue as v. 3. (b) EMET transliterates as "Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah" (more phonetically accurate); LOGOS/PNEUMA use the conventional English transliteration "Kibroth-hattaavah." The consensus follows the established English convention. (c) PNEUMA renders ha-mit'avvim as "who had been seized by craving" -- an interpretive expansion of the hitpael participle meaning "the ones craving." LOGOS's "who had the craving" is closer. (d) EMET has "the ones craving" (most literal participle).

---

### 11:35 -- "the people journeyed to Hazeroth" vs. "the people set out for Hazeroth" / "Khatserot" vs. "Hazeroth" (mi-Qivrot ha-Ta'avah nas'u ha-am Khatserot va-yihyu ba-Khatserot)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "From-Qivrot-ha-Ta'avah the-people journeyed to-Khatserot; and-they-were in-Khatserot." |
| LOGOS | "From Kibroth-hattaavah the people journeyed to Hazeroth, and they were at Hazeroth." |
| PNEUMA | "From Kibroth-hattaavah the people set out for Hazeroth, and they remained at Hazeroth." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "From Kibroth-hattaavah the people journeyed to Hazeroth, and they were at Hazeroth." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "set out for" for nas'u; EMET/LOGOS use "journeyed to." The Hebrew nasa means "to pull up stakes / journey / set out"; "journeyed" is the standard rendering. (b) PNEUMA renders va-yihyu ba-Khatserot as "they remained at Hazeroth"; the Hebrew simply says "they were at Hazeroth" (va-yihyu, "and they were"), not "they remained" (which would be va-yeshvu or va-yishsha'aru). (c) EMET transliterates as "Khatserot" (phonetic); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "Hazeroth" (conventional English). The consensus follows the established English convention.
